verdict: PASS — all six checks clear; one soft finding (undefined term 'sandboxed environment') surfaces for HITL awareness but does not reach the 3-finding escalation threshold for a hard fail.

citation_density.notes: All three resources ([1] IBM, [2] Martin Fowler, [3] The Register) appear in §5, §6, and §7. Primary [1] cited in §4 introduction and throughout §5–§7. No fabricated citations detected — all [1], [2], [3] markers resolve to declared resources.

soft_findings.issue: Term used in a defining clause ('runs it in a sandboxed environment') without a plain-language gloss. 'Sandboxed' is a computing security concept not introduced in any prior topic (4.1–4.4) and not listed in this topic's concepts_introduced. Used descriptively in one phrase only; the sandbox concept itself is not taught. Soft flag: add a brief parenthetical e.g. '(an isolated, safe environment)' on first use.

Product name used as an example label without definition. For absolute beginners 'plugin' may be unfamiliar. Used in a list of examples alongside Google Gemini and Anthropic's Claude; context makes the meaning inferable. Very minor.

length_sanity.notes: Standard corpus range 1500–6000 words. Estimated ~2,280 words. Within range. Corpus is appropriately longer than the reading artifact it will ground.

topic_scope.headline: Tool use — AI calling search, calculator, and code runner

soft_findings.issue: The phrase 'runs it in a sandboxed environment' introduces the computing concept of sandboxing descriptively without teaching it. This is borderline: it describes the code runner's operating environment without explaining what sandboxing means or why it matters. Not a hard scope_creep violation since the sandbox concept is not defined at depth; however, the undefined term creates a beginner readability gap (see beginner_readability check).

named_and_deferred_correctly.concept: exact format of the tool request / frameworks that manage multi-tool conversations
named_and_deferred_correctly.handling: Explicitly named and deferred with 'advanced topics you will encounter later'. Correct mention-and-defer form.
Referred back to 4.3 with '(Retrieval-Augmented Generation, covered in topic 4.3)'. Correct back-reference to prior topic; not re-taught here.

beginner_readability.result: PASS — 1 soft finding; escalation threshold (≥3 distinct findings) not reached

undefined_terms.detail: 'Sandboxed environment' appears in the code runner definition (§5.3) without a plain-language gloss. An absolute beginner will not know what 'sandboxed' means. Recommended fix: add parenthetical '(an isolated, safe environment that cannot affect the rest of the computer)'.

jargon_dump.detail: No paragraph introduces 3+ brand-new terms simultaneously. §5.2 Step 2 introduces 'structured request' and 'routing software' in the same step but each is explained individually in consecutive sentences. Below threshold.

academic_register.detail: No hedging phrases ('it is generally considered', 'many experts believe') detected. Sentences are predominantly short, active, and learner-addressed. Register is plain and confident throughout.

positive_observations: Every other new term is defined on first use with a plain-language gloss (search tool, calculator tool, code runner, structured request, routing software layer, tool-use cycle, tool availability scope).
Concrete everyday analogies lead each concept (sleeping friend analogy in §4, librarian form analogy in §5.5).
Tables used appropriately for comparisons (§5.1 gaps table, §5.4 before-and-after table).
LLM spelled out as 'LLM (Large Language Model)' on first use in §4.
Sentence lengths are consistently short; active voice and second-person address used throughout.

soft_findings_summary.recommended_fix: Add parenthetical gloss on first use: 'runs it in a sandboxed environment (an isolated, safe area that cannot affect the rest of the computer)'.
